> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBBBT604 - BIOINFORMATICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Microarray Data Analysis # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Microarray Data Analysis** is the systematic, multi-stage computational process of extracting biologically meaningful information from the high-dimensional, fluorescence-based intensity datasets generated by DNA/protein microarray hybridization experiments. It encompasses image quantification, background correction, normalization, statistical filtering for differential expression, multiple-testing correction, and downstream knowledge-mining through clustering, classification, and pathway enrichment — ultimately transforming raw spot intensities into ranked lists of biologically significant genes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 4):** Microarray technology, experimental design, two-color vs single-color arrays, image analysis, normalization algorithms (global, LOWESS, quantile), differential expression via fold-change and t-statistics, False Discovery Rate (FDR), and unsupervised clustering (Hierarchical, K-Means, SOM) are **core examinable concepts**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Library" Analogy Imagine a massive library with **tens of thousands of cookbooks** (one per gene), and you want to know which recipes were actually *cooked* last Tuesday versus last Thursday. - **The Microarray Slide** = A library shelf where every cookbook is pre-shelved in a fixed position (a "spot" with a known DNA sequence printed on glass). - **The mRNA Samples** = Two days' worth of kitchen leftovers, labeled with **red dye** (Day 1 — sick cell) and **green dye** (Day 2 — healthy cell). - **Hybridization** = You throw the labeled leftovers onto the shelf; each recipe finds its matching shelf position by base-pairing. - **The Laser Scanner** = A flashlight that makes red shelves glow if Day 1 leftovers bound, and green shelves glow if Day 2 leftovers bound. Yellow shelves = both days cooked equally. - **Microarray Data Analysis** = The librarian who reads every glowing shelf, removes the room's ambient light (background correction), adjusts for the flashlight's brightness quirks (normalization), and writes a report: *"On Tuesday, you cooked 3x more biryani and 5x less soup than on Thursday."* > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology Glossary (High-Yield for KTU):** > - **Probe** — A short, known DNA sequence immobilized on the slide. > - **Target** — The fluorescently-labeled sample cDNA/mRNA that hybridizes to the probe. > - **Spot** — A physical region on the array containing millions of copies of one probe. > - **Intensity (R, G)** — Raw fluorescence signals from the red and green channels. > - **Log-ratio (M)** — The base-2 logarithm of the red/green intensity ratio. > - **Gene Expression Matrix** — Genes × Samples matrix that is the output of the entire pipeline. ## 1.3 Types of Microarrays (Quick Reference) | Type | Platform | Channels | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | cDNA microarray | Spotted cDNA clones | **Two-color** (Cy3/Cy5) | Comparative expression | | Oligonucleotide array | Short synthetic oligos (25–70 mer) | One or two-color | Gene expression profiling | | GeneChip (Affymetrix) | 25-mer oligos photolithographically synthesized | **Single-color** (biotin/streptavidin-PE) | High-throughput expression | | SNP array | Allele-specific oligos | Single-color | Genotyping, GWAS | | Comparative Genomic Hybridization (CGH) array | BAC / oligo probes | Two-color | Copy-number variation | | Protein microarray | Antibodies / antigens | Fluorescent | Proteomics, biomarker discovery | > [!NOTE] > Affymetrix GeneChips and Agilent single-color arrays dominate modern research. **Two-color cDNA arrays** are still heavily emphasized in KTU theory questions because they introduce the elegant R/G log-ratio framework. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Canonical Microarray Data Analysis Pipeline The pipeline is universally taught as a **seven-stage workflow**. Each stage removes a specific source of technical noise to reveal the true biological signal. ### Stage 1 — Image Acquisition & Quantification - The hybridized slide is scanned at high resolution by a **laser confocal scanner** (e.g., Affymetrix 3000 7G, Agilent G2505C). - The scanner outputs a **16-bit TIFF image** where pixel intensity is proportional to bound fluorophore concentration. - Software (GenePix, ImaGene, Agilent Feature Extraction) segments the image into a **grid**, identifies each spot, and computes: - **Median pixel intensity** of the spot (signal) - **Median pixel intensity** of the local background (noise) - Output: A raw intensity table — columns = `GeneID, Rforeground, Gforeground, Rbackground, Gbackground`. ### Stage 2 — Background Correction - Raw intensities include non-specific fluorescence (auto-fluorescence of glass, residual unbound dye). - **Subtraction method:** $I_{\text{corrected}} = I_{\text{foreground}} - I_{\text{background}}$. - **More robust method (model-based):** Edwards' method, RMA's PM-only model — assumes background follows an exponential or normal distribution and corrects probabilistically. - *Caveat:* Negative values after subtraction are typically floored to a small positive number (e.g., **1**) before log-transformation, because $\log_2(0)$ is undefined. ### Stage 3 — Normalization The **single most important and most-asked step in KTU exams**. Normalization removes systematic biases that affect all spots equally (dye bias, print-tip effects, spatial gradients). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three canonical normalization methods every KTU student must know:** **(a) Global Normalization (Linear Scaling)** - Assumes the average R/G ratio across all genes on the array should be 1.0 (no global differential expression). - Compute the scaling factor $c = \dfrac{\sum_{i} R_i}{\sum_{i} G_i}$. - Normalized values: $R_i^{\text{norm}} = \dfrac{R_i}{c}$, $G_i^{\text{norm}} = G_i$. **(b) LOESS / LOWESS Normalization (Local Regression)** - Used in **two-color arrays** to remove intensity-dependent dye bias. - Plots **M (log-ratio)** vs **A (mean log-intensity)** for all genes → this is the **MA-plot**. - A LOWESS curve is fitted through the scatter. The curve captures the systematic bias; each point is shifted vertically so the curve becomes a horizontal line at $M = 0$. - $M_i^{\text{norm}} = M_i - f_{\text{loess}}(A_i)$. **(c) Quantile Normalization** - The **gold standard** for Affymetrix single-color data (RMA pipeline). - Forces the empirical distribution of intensities to be **identical across all arrays**. - Algorithm: 1. Sort each column of the intensity matrix independently. 2. Compute the row-wise mean across all sorted columns. 3. Replace each entry by the mean of its rank across columns. 4. Re-order columns back to original gene order. ### Stage 4 — Log Transformation - All downstream analysis is performed in $\log_2$ space. - $M_i = \log_2(R_i^{\text{norm}}) - \log_2(G_i^{\text{norm}}) = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{R_i^{\text{norm}}}{G_i^{\text{norm}}}\right)$. - **Why?** Fold-changes become symmetric: a 2-fold up-regulation ($M = +1$) and 2-fold down-regulation ($M = -1$) are equidistant from zero. Statistical tests assume normality; log-transformed intensities approximate normality. ### Stage 5 — Filtering for Differential Expression Two complementary criteria: - **Fold Change (FC):** $FC_i = 2^{M_i}$. Threshold typically $FC \geq 2$ (i.e., $\vert M \vert \geq 1$). - **Statistical Significance:** Compute a per-gene p-value using a **modified t-test** (see Stage 6). - **Combined criterion (KTU-favorite):** Select genes where $\vert M_i \vert \geq 1$ **AND** $p_i \leq 0.05$. ### Stage 6 — Statistical Inference Because microarray data has **low replicates** (typically $n = 3$ biological replicates), the ordinary Student's t-test is unreliable. Variants used: - **CyberT / Regularized t-test:** Borrows information across genes with similar expression to stabilize the variance estimate. - **SAM (Significance Analysis of Microarrays):** Adds a small constant $s_0$ to the denominator to avoid inflated t-statistics when variance is near zero. - **Empirical Bayes (limma):** The most widely used (in Bioconductor). Moderates the standard error using a prior distribution fitted across all genes. ### Stage 7 — Multiple Testing Correction When you test **20,000 genes simultaneously** at $\alpha = 0.05$, you expect **1,000 false positives by chance alone**. Multiple testing correction is mandatory. - **Bonferroni:** $\alpha_{\text{adjusted}} = \dfrac{\alpha}{m}$, where $m$ = number of tests. Very conservative. - **Benjamini-Hochberg FDR:** Controls the *expected proportion* of false discoveries among the rejected hypotheses. $FDR = E\!\left[\dfrac{V}{R \;\vert\; R > 0}\right]$ where $V$ = false positives, $R$ = rejected nulls. - Typical KTU threshold: $FDR \leq 0.05$. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Meaning / Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Background subtraction | $I_{\text{adj}} = I_{\text{fg}} - I_{\text{bg}}$ | Remove non-specific fluorescence | | 2 | Log-ratio (M-value) | $M = \log_2(R/G)$ | Symmetric fold-change measure | | 3 | Mean log-intensity (A-value) | $A = \dfrac{1}{2}(\log_2 R + \log_2 G)$ | Overall brightness of a spot | | 4 | Global scaling factor | $c = \dfrac{\sum R_i}{\sum G_i}$ | Equalize total red/green signal | | 5 | LOESS normalization | $M_i^{\text{norm}} = M_i - f(A_i)$ | Remove intensity-dependent dye bias | | 6 | Fold change | $FC = 2^{\vert M \vert}$ | Biological magnitude of change | | 7 | Linear fold change (raw) | $FC_{\text{raw}} = R/G$ | Multiplicative change (raw scale) | | 8 | CyberT t-statistic | $t = \dfrac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{s_{\text{reg}}}$ | With regularized variance $s_{\text{reg}}^2 = \dfrac{d_0 s_0^2 + d s^2}{d_0 + d}$ | | 9 | SAM statistic | $d(i) = \dfrac{\bar{x}_1(i) - \bar{x}_2(i)}{s(i) + s_0}$ | Stabilized t-statistic | | 10 | Bonferroni threshold | $\alpha_{\text{adj}} = \alpha / m$ | Family-wise error control | | 11 | BH-FDR adjusted p-value | $p_{(i)}^{\text{adj}} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{p_{(i)} \cdot m}{i},\; 1\right)$ | Adjusted in ascending order | | 12 | Euclidean distance (clustering) | $d(x,y) = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{n} (x_k - y_k)^2}$ | Distance between gene profiles | | 13 | Pearson correlation | $r = \dfrac{\sum (x_k - \bar{x})(y_k - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_k - \bar{x})^2 \sum (y_k - \bar{y})^2}}$ | Shape-similarity of expression profiles | | 14 | Z-score standardization | $z_{ik} = \dfrac{x_{ik} - \mu_k}{\sigma_k}$ | Mean-center and scale by gene $k$ | | 15 | Silhouette coefficient | $s(i) = \dfrac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max[a(i), b(i)]}$ | Cluster quality (range $-1$ to $+1$) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Microarray analysis pipelines form the conceptual backbone of all modern **omics** — RNA-seq differential expression analysis (DESeq2, edgeR) is a direct descendant of the same statistical framework (variance shrinkage + multiple-testing correction). Mastery of these formulas is **transferable** to genomics, clinical biomarker discovery, and pharmacogenomics. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example — End-to-End Differential Expression > [!NOTE] > **Scenario (Two-Color cDNA Array):** A researcher compares a tumor sample (Cy5 = **R**) against a normal reference (Cy3 = **G**) on a single microarray slide, with **3 biological replicates** ($n = 3$). Below are the raw intensities for the gene **BRCA1** on all 6 arrays (3 R, 3 G), captured by GenePix. Background intensities are also given. Compute the fold change, perform a t-test, and decide whether BRCA1 is differentially expressed. | Replicate | $R_{\text{fg}}$ | $R_{\text{bg}}$ | $G_{\text{fg}}$ | $G_{\text{bg}}$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 1840 | 220 | 980 | 200 | | 2 | 2120 | 240 | 1020 | 210 | | 3 | 1990 | 230 | 950 | 195 | ### Step 1 — Background Subtraction Apply $I_{\text{adj}} = I_{\text{fg}} - I_{\text{bg}}$ to each replicate: $$ \begin{aligned} R_1^{\text{adj}} &= 1840 - 220 = 1620 \\ R_2^{\text{adj}} &= 2120 - 240 = 1880 \\ R_3^{\text{adj}} &= 1990 - 230 = 1760 \\ G_1^{\text{adj}} &= 980 - 200 = 780 \\ G_2^{\text{adj}} &= 1020 - 210 = 810 \\ G_3^{\text{adj}} &= 950 - 195 = 755 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Log Transformation Convert each adjusted intensity to $\log_2$: $$ \begin{aligned} \log_2(R_1) &= \log_2(1620) \approx 10.662 \\ \log_2(R_2) &= \log_2(1880) \approx 10.876 \\ \log_2(R_3) &= \log_2(1760) \approx 10.781 \\ \log_2(G_1) &= \log_2(780) \approx 9.607 \\ \log_2(G_2) &= \log_2(810) \approx 9.662 \\ \log_2(G_3) &= \log_2(755) \approx 9.561 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute M and A per Replicate $M_i = \log_2(R_i) - \log_2(G_i)$, $A_i = \dfrac{1}{2}(\log_2(R_i) + \log_2(G_i))$: $$ \begin{aligned} M_1 &= 10.662 - 9.607 = 1.055 \\ M_2 &= 10.876 - 9.662 = 1.214 \\ M_3 &= 10.781 - 9.561 = 1.220 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} A_1 &= (10.662 + 9.607)/2 = 10.135 \\ A_2 &= (10.876 + 9.662)/2 = 10.269 \\ A_3 &= (10.781 + 9.561)/2 = 10.171 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Compute Mean M, Fold Change $$ \bar{M} = \dfrac{1.055 + 1.214 + 1.220}{3} = \dfrac{3.489}{3} = 1.163 $$ $$ FC = 2^{\bar{M}} = 2^{1.163} \approx 2.244 $$ **Interpretation:** BRCA1 is approximately **2.24-fold up-regulated** in tumor vs. normal on the $\log_2$ scale. ### Step 5 — Compute Standard Deviation of M $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_M &= \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (M_i - \bar{M})^2}{n - 1}} \\ &= \sqrt{\dfrac{(1.055 - 1.163)^2 + (1.214 - 1.163)^2 + (1.220 - 1.163)^2}{3 - 1}} \\ &= \sqrt{\dfrac{0.01166 + 0.00260 + 0.00325}{2}} \\ &= \sqrt{\dfrac{0.01751}{2}} = \sqrt{0.008755} \approx 0.0936 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Perform One-Sample t-Test Against $\mu_0 = 0$ Degrees of freedom: $df = n - 1 = 2$. $$ t = \dfrac{\bar{M} - 0}{s_M / \sqrt{n}} = \dfrac{1.163}{0.0936 / \sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{1.163}{0.0540} \approx 21.54 $$ For $df = 2$, the critical $t$ value at $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed) is **4.303**. Since $21.54 \gg 4.303$, the result is highly significant. ### Step 7 — Multiple Testing Correction Suppose this gene is one of $m = 20{,}000$ genes tested. **Bonferroni threshold:** $$ \alpha_{\text{adj}} = \dfrac{0.05}{20{,}000} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} $$ The raw p-value corresponding to $t = 21.54$ with $df = 2$ is approximately $p \approx 0.0021$. **Bonferroni fails** ($0.0021 > 2.5 \times 10^{-6}$). However, applying **Benjamini-Hochberg FDR** at $q = 0.05$, the gene easily passes since FDR is far less stringent. > [!NOTE] > **Decision:** BRCA1 is **differentially expressed** — fold change $\geq 2$ ✓ AND FDR-adjusted $p \leq 0.05$ ✓. ## 3.2 Worked Example — Benjamini-Hochberg FDR Computation Consider a small toy experiment with **$m = 5$ genes** and their raw p-values: $p = (0.001, 0.008, 0.039, 0.041, 0.042, 0.060, 0.074, 0.205)$. Target FDR $q = 0.05$. ### Step 1 — Sort p-values in ascending order | Rank $i$ | $p_{(i)}$ | |---|---| | 1 | 0.001 | | 2 | 0.008 | | 3 | 0.039 | | 4 | 0.041 | | 5 | 0.042 | | 6 | 0.060 | | 7 | 0.074 | | 8 | 0.205 | ### Step 2 — Compute Adjusted p-values The BH formula is $p_{(i)}^{\text{adj}} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{p_{(i)} \cdot m}{i},\; 1\right)$, then enforce monotonicity (take cumulative minimum from the bottom up). $$ \begin{aligned} i=1: \quad &\dfrac{0.001 \times 8}{1} = 0.008 \\ i=2: \quad &\dfrac{0.008 \times 8}{2} = 0.032 \\ i=3: \quad &\dfrac{0.039 \times 8}{3} = 0.104 \\ i=4: \quad &\dfrac{0.041 \times 8}{4} = 0.082 \\ i=5: \quad &\dfrac{0.042 \times 8}{5} = 0.0672 \\ i=6: \quad &\dfrac{0.060 \times 8}{6} = 0.0800 \\ i=7: \quad &\dfrac{0.074 \times 8}{7} = 0.0846 \\ i=8: \quad &\dfrac{0.205 \times 8}{8} = 0.2050 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Enforce Monotonicity (cumulative minimum from bottom) $$ \begin{aligned} q_8 &= 0.2050 \\ q_7 &= \min(0.0846,\; 0.2050) = 0.0846 \\ q_6 &= \min(0.0800,\; 0.0846) = 0.0800 \\ q_5 &= \min(0.0672,\; 0.0800) = 0.0672 \\ q_4 &= \min(0.0820,\; 0.0672) = 0.0672 \\ q_3 &= \min(0.1040,\; 0.0672) = 0.0672 \\ q_2 &= \min(0.0320,\; 0.0672) = 0.0320 \\ q_1 &= \min(0.0080,\; 0.0320) = 0.0080 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Identify Significant Genes Select genes where $q_i \leq 0.05$: **Gene 1 ($q = 0.008$) and Gene 2 ($q = 0.032$) are significant.** Gene 3 onward all exceed $q = 0.05$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why FDR is preferred over Bonferroni in microarrays:** Bonferroni assumes a single false positive is unacceptable (Family-Wise Error Rate control). Microarray analysis tolerates a small fraction of false discoveries in exchange for higher *statistical power* — exactly what FDR delivers. ## 3.3 Worked Example — Quantile Normalization on 3 Arrays × 4 Genes Initial intensity matrix $X$ (rows = genes, columns = arrays): | Gene | Array 1 | Array 2 | Array 3 | |---|---|---|---| | G1 | 5 | 4 | 3 | | G2 | 2 | 8 | 6 | | G3 | 7 | 1 | 5 | | G4 | 4 | 9 | 2 | ### Step 1 — Sort Each Column Independently $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Array 1 sorted:} & \quad [2, 4, 5, 7] \\ \text{Array 2 sorted:} & \quad [1, 4, 8, 9] \\ \text{Array 3 sorted:} & \quad [2, 3, 5, 6] \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute Row-Wise Means of Sorted Columns $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Row 1 mean} &= (2 + 1 + 2)/3 = 5/3 \approx 1.667 \\ \text{Row 2 mean} &= (4 + 4 + 3)/3 = 11/3 \approx 3.667 \\ \text{Row 3 mean} &= (5 + 8 + 5)/3 = 18/3 = 6.000 \\ \text{Row 4 mean} &= (7 + 9 + 6)/3 = 22/3 \approx 7.333 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Replace by Rank-Mean The original rank of each entry in its column determines which row-mean is substituted. The result is that **all three columns will be identical after normalization**, with values $[1.667, 3.667, 6.000, 7.333]$ assigned according to each gene's original rank within its column. > [!NOTE] > Quantile normalization is the cornerstone of the **RMA (Robust Multi-array Average)** pipeline used universally for Affymetrix data. It is **non-parametric**, makes no distributional assumptions, and is computationally $O(mn\log n)$. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Microarray Data Analysis — End-to-End Pipeline (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Biological Samples
Tumor vs Normal"] --> B["RNA Extraction
and Quality Check"] B --> C["cDNA Synthesis
with Cy5 Cy3 Dye Labeling"] C --> D["Hybridization
on Microarray Slide"] D --> E["Laser Scanner
Image Acquisition 16-bit TIFF"] E --> F["Grid Alignment
and Spot Segmentation"] F --> G["Background Correction
Subtraction or Model Based"] G --> H["Normalization
Global LOWESS or Quantile"] H --> I["Log2 Transformation
and MA Plot Generation"] I --> J["Differential Expression
Fold Change and t Test"] J --> K["Multiple Testing Correction
Bonferroni or BH FDR"] K --> L["Significant Gene List
Filtered Output"] L --> M["Clustering Analysis
Hierarchical K Means SOM"] L --> N["Heatmap Visualization
and PCA"] L --> O["Pathway Enrichment
GO and KEGG Analysis"] M --> P["Biological Interpretation
and Hypothesis Generation"] N --> P O --> P ``` ## 4.2 Two-Color vs Single-Color Experimental Design ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TwoColor["TWO COLOR cDNA ARRAY"] T1["Tumor mRNA
CY5 Red Label"] --> HYB1["Hybridize on
Same Slide"] N1["Normal mRNA
CY3 Green Label"] --> HYB1 HYB1 --> SCAN1["Single Image
R and G Channels"] SCAN1 --> RATIO["Compute R/G
Log2 Ratio M"] end subgraph SingleColor["SINGLE COLOR Affymetrix GeneChip"] T2["Tumor mRNA
Biotin Label"] --> HYB2["Hybridize on
Slide A"] N2["Normal mRNA
Biotin Label"] --> HYB3["Hybridize on
Slide B"] HYB2 --> SCAN2["Image A"] HYB3 --> SCAN3["Image B"] SCAN2 --> QN["Quantile Normalize
Across Arrays"] SCAN3 --> QN QN --> COMP["Compare Intensities
Across Slides"] end ``` ## 4.3 Hierarchical Clustering of Genes ```mermaid flowchart TD DATA["Expression Matrix
Genes x Samples"] --> DIST["Compute Pairwise
Distance Matrix
Euclidean or Pearson"] DIST --> LINK["Apply Linkage
Criterion Average Complete Ward"] LINK --> DEND["Generate Dendrogram
Hierarchical Tree"] DEND --> CUT["Cut Dendrogram
at Chosen Height k"] CUT --> CLUST["Cluster Assignment
Cluster 1, 2, ..., k"] CLUST --> VAL["Validate Clusters
Silhouette Coefficient"] VAL --> BIO["Biological Annotation
GO Term Enrichment"] ``` ## 4.4 K-Means Clustering Iterative Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD INIT["Step 1 Initialize k
Random Centroids"] --> ASSIGN["Step 2 Assign Each Gene
to Nearest Centroid"] ASSIGN --> UPDATE["Step 3 Recompute
Centroids as Cluster Means"] UPDATE --> CHECK{"Step 4 Converged
Centroids Stable"} CHECK -- No --> ASSIGN CHECK -- Yes --> OUTPUT["Step 5 Final Clusters
and Membership Matrix"] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading these diagrams in KTU exams:** When a question asks "Draw/Explain the workflow", use **rectangular process nodes**, **diamond decision nodes**, and **clear arrows** for data flow. Always label data products (e.g., "MA-plot", "distance matrix") on the connecting edges. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Define Microarray. Differentiate between a one-color and a two-color microarray.** **Model Answer:** A microarray is a solid substrate (glass slide or silicon chip) on which thousands of DNA sequences (probes) are immobilized in a grid pattern at known locations, used to measure the expression levels of thousands of genes simultaneously through fluorescent hybridization. | Feature | One-Color Microarray | Two-Color Microarray | |---|---|---| | Samples per slide | One sample only | Two samples co-hybridized | | Dye used | Single fluorophore (e.g., biotin-streptavidin-PE) | Two fluorophores (Cy3 = green, Cy5 = red) | | Reference sample | Not required on the slide; cross-slide comparison | Required for normalization | | Comparison | Across different arrays (after normalization) | Direct on-slide R/G ratio | | Example platform | Affymetrix GeneChip | Agilent cDNA / oligonucleotide | | Output quantity | Absolute intensity per gene | Log-ratio (M-value) per gene | **(1 mark: definition, 1 mark: one-color features, 1 mark: two-color features)** --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: What is Multiple Testing Problem in microarray data analysis? Explain Benjamini-Hochberg FDR.** **Model Answer:** When 20,000 genes are tested individually at $\alpha = 0.05$, the expected number of false positives is $20{,}000 \times 0.05 = 1000$, even if no gene is truly differentially expressed. This is the **Multiple Testing Problem**. The **Benjamini-Hochberg FDR procedure** controls the expected proportion of false discoveries among rejected hypotheses: 1. Sort p-values in ascending order: $p_{(1)} \leq p_{(2)} \leq \cdots \leq p_{(m)}$. 2. Compute adjusted p-value: $p_{(i)}^{\text{adj}} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{p_{(i)} \cdot m}{i},\; 1\right)$ (with cumulative-minimum monotonicity from the largest rank downward). 3. Reject all hypotheses where $p_{(i)}^{\text{adj}} \leq q$ (the desired FDR level, typically 0.05). FDR is **less stringent** than Bonferroni and **more powerful** — it is therefore the standard in microarray studies. **(1 mark: problem statement, 2 marks: BH-FDR algorithm with formula)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Describe the major steps involved in Microarray Data Analysis with a neat pipeline diagram. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** A canonical microarray data analysis pipeline comprises the following steps: 1. **Image Acquisition:** A laser confocal scanner excites the fluorophores (Cy3/Cy5 or streptavidin-PE) and captures a high-resolution 16-bit TIFF image. The scanner software segments the image into a grid, identifies each spot, and reports foreground/background pixel intensities for both channels (R, G). 2. **Background Correction:** Non-specific fluorescence is removed by subtracting local background from foreground intensity: $I_{\text{adj}} = I_{\text{fg}} - I_{\text{bg}}$. Robust model-based methods (RMA, Edwards) are preferred for Affymetrix data. Negative values are floored to a small positive constant (e.g., 1). 3. **Normalization:** Removes systematic biases. For two-color arrays: **Global normalization** (single scaling factor) or **LOESS normalization** on the MA-plot to remove intensity-dependent dye bias. For Affymetrix arrays: **Quantile normalization** forces identical empirical intensity distributions across arrays. 4. **Log-Transformation:** All intensities are converted to $\log_2$ so that up- and down-regulation are symmetric and the data approximate normality. 5. **Filtering for Differential Expression:** Genes are ranked by **fold change** ($FC = 2^{\vert M \vert} \geq 2$) **and/or** a per-gene p-value from a t-test (or its regularized variants: CyberT, SAM, limma). 6. **Multiple Testing Correction:** Apply **Bonferroni** (strict) or **Benjamini-Hochberg FDR** (preferred) to control false discoveries. 7. **Clustering and Visualization:** Group co-expressed genes using **Hierarchical clustering**, **K-Means**, or **SOM**, and visualize results via **heatmaps** and **MA-plots**. **Pipeline Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Image"] --> B["Background"] B --> C["Normalize"] C --> D["Log2"] D --> E["Statistics"] E --> F["FDR Filter"] F --> G["Cluster and Visualize"] ``` **(Marks distribution: 1 mark per step × 7 = 7 marks, with diagram carrying separate credit)** --- **(b)** Explain the **MA-plot** in detail. How is it used in **LOESS normalization**? **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** The MA-plot is a 2-D diagnostic scatter plot used to visualize the relationship between the **magnitude of change** (M) and the **average intensity** (A) of each gene on a two-color microarray. - **M-axis (y-axis):** $\;M = \log_2(R) - \log_2(G)\;$ — the log-fold-change (R over G). - **A-axis (x-axis):** $\;A = \dfrac{1}{2}(\log_2 R + \log_2 G)\;$ — the average brightness (overall expression level). **Interpretation:** - Genes with $M = 0$ lie on the horizontal axis — they are equally expressed in both samples. - Genes above the axis ($M > 0$) are up-regulated in R; genes below ($M < 0$) are down-regulated. - A symmetric "bow-tie" or curved cloud around $M = 0$ indicates no major bias. A systematic curve (e.g., upward bow at low A) indicates **intensity-dependent dye bias**. **LOESS Normalization using the MA-plot:** 1. Compute $(A_i, M_i)$ for every gene. 2. Fit a **LOWESS curve** $f(A)$ through the scatter (locally weighted polynomial regression). 3. The fitted curve $f(A)$ represents the systematic bias as a function of intensity. 4. Correct each gene: $M_i^{\text{norm}} = M_i - f(A_i)$. 5. After correction, the LOWESS curve becomes a horizontal line at $M = 0$, indicating the bias has been removed. **Why LOESS works:** It is **non-parametric** and **local** — it adapts to the shape of the bias without assuming a specific functional form. The bandwidth parameter (typically $f = 0.2$ to $0.5$) controls smoothness. **Significance for KTU:** MA-plot is the **single most-used diagnostic** in two-color array analysis. KTU examiners frequently test whether students can interpret the bias direction and explain the correction geometry. **(Marks distribution: 2 marks definition, 2 marks interpretation, 3 marks LOESS correction steps)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Define **Hierarchical Clustering**. Explain the **agglomerative algorithm** with a suitable example. Mention any two **linkage methods**. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Hierarchical clustering is an unsupervised learning method that builds a hierarchy of nested clusters represented as a **dendrogram** (tree diagram). It does not require pre-specifying the number of clusters. **Agglomerative (Bottom-Up) Algorithm:** 1. Start with $N$ singleton clusters — each data point is its own cluster. 2. Compute the **pairwise distance matrix** using Euclidean distance, Pearson correlation, or Manhattan distance. 3. **Merge** the two closest clusters according to a chosen **linkage criterion**. 4. Update the distance matrix to reflect the new cluster. 5. Repeat steps 3–4 until a single cluster containing all points remains. 6. Cut the dendrogram at a chosen height $h$ to obtain $k$ clusters. **Worked Example (4 genes A, B, C, D; Euclidean distances):** Initial distances: $$ \begin{aligned} d(A,B) &= 2,\quad d(A,C) = 6,\quad d(A,D) = 10 \\ d(B,C) &= 5,\quad d(B,D) = 9,\quad d(C,D) = 4 \end{aligned} $$ Merge closest pair: **A–B** (distance 2). New cluster {A,B}. Update distances (using single linkage): - $d(\{A,B\}, C) = \min(6, 5) = 5$ - $d(\{A,B\}, D) = \min(10, 9) = 9$ - $d(C, D) = 4$ Merge next closest pair: **C–D** (distance 4). New cluster {C,D}. Update: $d(\{A,B\}, \{C,D\}) = \min(5, 9, 5, 9) = 5$. Final merge yields a single tree. **Two Linkage Methods:** | Method | Formula | Behavior | |---|---|---| | **Single linkage** | $d(C_i, C_j) = \min_{x \in C_i, y \in C_j} d(x,y)$ | Tends to produce long, chain-like clusters | | **Complete linkage** | $d(C_i, C_j) = \max_{x \in C_i, y \in C_j} d(x,y)$ | Produces compact, spherical clusters | | (Average linkage) | $d = \text{mean of pairwise distances}$ | Compromise between the two | | (Ward's linkage) | Minimizes within-cluster variance | Preferred for expression data | **(Marks distribution: 2 marks definition + algorithm, 2 marks worked example, 3 marks linkage methods)** --- **(b)** Explain **K-Means Clustering Algorithm**. Compare K-Means and Hierarchical Clustering. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **K-Means Algorithm (MacQueen, 1967):** **Input:** Dataset $X = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$, number of clusters $k$. **Steps:** 1. **Initialize** $k$ centroids randomly (e.g., by picking $k$ random data points, or using K-Means++ smart seeding). 2. **Assignment step:** Assign each $x_i$ to the cluster whose centroid is closest (typically by squared Euclidean distance): $$C^{(t)}(i) = \arg\min_{j \in \{1,\ldots,k\}} \;\Vert x_i - \mu_j^{(t)} \Vert^2$$ 3. **Update step:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of all points assigned to its cluster: $$\mu_j^{(t+1)} = \dfrac{1}{\vert C_j^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{i : C^{(t)}(i) = j} x_i$$ 4. **Convergence check:** If centroids did not change (or changed below threshold $\epsilon$), stop. Otherwise, go to step 2. 5. **Output:** Final cluster assignments $C^{(t)}$ and centroids $\mu^{(t)}$. **Objective function minimized (Within-Cluster Sum of Squares, WCSS):** $$J = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{i : C(i) = j} \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^2$$ **Comparison Table:** | Aspect | K-Means | Hierarchical | |---|---|---| | Number of clusters $k$ | Must be pre-specified | Determined post-hoc by cutting the dendrogram | | Computational complexity | $O(nkt)$ per iteration | $O(n^3)$ naive, $O(n^2 \log n)$ optimized | | Cluster shape | Spherical, equal-variance | Arbitrary (depends on linkage) | | Determinism | Random initialization → different results | Deterministic (given distance metric) | | Scalability | Excellent for large $n$ | Limited to a few thousand points | | Best use | Large gene-expression datasets | Small datasets, exploratory visualization | | Sensitivity to outliers | High (centroids are means) | Lower (linkage methods are robust) | **Choosing $k$:** Use the **Elbow Method** (plot WCSS vs $k$; look for the elbow) or the **Silhouette Coefficient** ($s(i) = (b(i) - a(i))/\max(a(i), b(i))$). **(Marks distribution: 3 marks K-Means steps + objective, 2 marks formulas, 2 marks comparison table)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to log-transform before averaging.** Fold-change averaging must be done on $\log_2$ scale, not raw intensities. Raw averaging is dominated by high-intensity spots. > 2. **Reporting only fold-change without a p-value.** KTU requires both biological magnitude (FC) and statistical significance (p/FDR). A gene with FC = 10 but p = 0.4 is noise. > 3. **Confusing Bonferroni with FDR.** Bonferroni = Family-Wise Error Rate (very strict). FDR = expected false-discovery proportion (lenient). Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks. > 4. **Drawing dendrograms as flowcharts.** A dendrogram is a **tree** with vertical lines and T-junctions. Students who draw it as a flowchart lose marks on diagram questions. > 5. **Skipping the global normalization scaling-factor derivation.** Examiners love asking "compute $c$ for a given table" — practice this! > 6. **Mixing M and A definitions.** $M = \log_2(R/G)$ is **log-ratio**; $A = \frac{1}{2}(\log_2 R + \log_2 G)$ is **average intensity**. Swapping definitions is a guaranteed 1-mark loss. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Microarray Data Analysis** - [ ] **Microarray** is a high-throughput hybridization-based assay measuring thousands of genes in parallel; **one-color** = absolute intensity, **two-color** = comparative R/G log-ratio. - [ ] **Pipeline:** Image → Background Correction → Normalization → Log₂ → Statistics → Multiple Testing Correction → Clustering → Interpretation. - [ ] **Background correction** = subtract local background intensity; negative values floored to 1. - [ ] **Global normalization** uses a single scaling factor $c = \sum R_i / \sum G_i$. - [ ] **LOESS normalization** fits a curve on the **MA-plot** and subtracts it; removes intensity-dependent dye bias. - [ ] **Quantile normalization** (RMA) makes the empirical intensity distribution identical across all arrays. - [ ] **MA-plot:** $M = \log_2(R/G)$ (y-axis, fold-change), $A = \tfrac{1}{2}(\log_2 R + \log_2 G)$ (x-axis, brightness). - [ ] **Fold change** $FC = 2^{|M|}$; threshold usually $FC \geq 2$ ($|M| \geq 1$). - [ ] **t-statistic** for differential expression: ordinary t-test is unstable for $n = 3$; use **CyberT**, **SAM**, or **limma** for variance stabilization. - [ ] **SAM statistic:** $d(i) = (\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2)/(s(i) + s_0)$, where $s_0$ is a fudge factor. - [ ] **Bonferroni** = $\alpha/m$ (very strict); **Benjamini-Hochberg FDR** = $p_{(i)}^{\text{adj}} = \min(p_{(i)} m/i, 1)$ with cumulative-min monotonicity (preferred). - [ ] **Hierarchical clustering** = agglomerative bottom-up; outputs a dendrogram; linkage = single/complete/average/Ward's. - [ ] **K-Means** alternates between assignment and centroid update until convergence; minimizes WCSS. - [ ] **Distance metrics:** Euclidean (magnitude), Pearson correlation (shape). - [ ] **Z-score standardization** = $(x - \mu)/\sigma$ — used before heatmap rendering for visual contrast. - [ ] **Silhouette coefficient** $s(i) \in [-1, 1]$ measures cluster cohesion + separation; closer to +1 = better. - [ ] **Heatmaps** visualize the gene-expression matrix with hierarchical clustering on both rows (genes) and columns (samples). - [ ] **PCA** is often used as a complementary visualization to detect outlier samples and batch effects. - [ ] **Engineering bridge:** The same statistical pipeline (variance shrinkage + FDR) underlies modern **RNA-seq** tools (DESeq2, edgeR) — these concepts are **transferable**. - [ ] **Reproducibility mantra:** Always log-transform → normalize → filter → correct → cluster → interpret. **Never skip the FDR step.**

topics.title: Gene Expression Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBBT604 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gene Expression Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Gene Expression Analysis** is the systematic study of the quantitative and qualitative measurement of the transcription products of genes (primarily mRNA) within a cell, tissue, or organism under specific physiological, developmental, or pathological conditions. It aims to determine **which genes are expressed**, **to what extent**, **when**, and **under what regulatory conditions**, providing a molecular snapshot of cellular functional state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Gene expression analysis refers to the suite of high-throughput bioinformatics techniques used to measure, compare, and interpret the abundance of mRNA transcripts (and in advanced contexts, proteins and non-coding RNAs) across biological samples using technologies such as **DNA Microarrays**, **RNA-Seq (Next-Generation Sequencing)**, **qRT-PCR**, and **SAGE (Serial Analysis of Gene Expression)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a **large industrial factory** with thousands of machines (genes). Each machine produces a specific product (protein/mRNA). To understand which machines are working hard, which are idle, and which are broken, you install a **sound meter** on each machine and measure its noise level.

- **High noise** = Gene is highly expressed (turned ON)
- **Low noise** = Gene is weakly expressed
- **Silence** = Gene is silenced (turned OFF)
- **Different time points** = Comparing disease vs. healthy, treated vs. untreated

Gene expression analysis is the **"noise meter"** of the cell — it tells us the **activity level of every gene simultaneously** in a single experiment, giving us a global picture of the cell's behavior.

### 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Units

> [!NOTE]
> The most critical numerical concepts you MUST memorize:
> - **RPKM** (Reads Per Kilobase per Million mapped reads) — for **single-end** RNA-Seq
> - **FPKM** (Fragments Per Kilobase per Million mapped reads) — for **paired-end** RNA-Seq
> - **TPM** (Transcripts Per Million) — the modern, length-normalized replacement for RPKM/FPKM
> - **Fold Change (FC)** — typically a value of **2.0 (up)** or **0.5 (down)** is the biological significance threshold
> - **p-value / adjusted p-value (FDR)** — statistical significance threshold is typically **0.05**
> - **Log₂ Fold Change** — values of **+1 (2× up)** or **−1 (2× down)** are commonly used cutoffs

### 1.4 Two Major Experimental Platforms

| Platform | Principle | Output |
|----------|-----------|--------|
| **DNA Microarray** | Hybridization of labeled cDNA to pre-designed probes on a chip | Fluorescence intensity |
| **RNA-Seq** | High-throughput sequencing of cDNA fragments | Read counts |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The General Workflow of Gene Expression Analysis

The pipeline follows a logical sequence of operations that must be understood step-by-step:

1. **Sample Preparation & RNA Extraction**
   - Isolate total RNA from biological samples (e.g., tumor vs. normal tissue).
   - Quality check using **RIN (RNA Integrity Number)** — must be **≥ 7.0** for reliable results.

2. **Library Construction & Sequencing / Hybridization**
   - Convert mRNA to cDNA.
   - For microarrays: hybridize to a chip.
   - For RNA-Seq: fragment, ligate adapters, sequence using NGS platforms (Illumina, Ion Torrent).

3. **Raw Data Acquisition**
   - Microarray: image scanned → raw **intensity values** per probe.
   - RNA-Seq: **FASTQ files** containing sequence reads + quality scores (Phred Q-scores).

4. **Quality Control (QC)**
   - FASTQC, Fastp, Trimmomatic for RNA-Seq.
   - Remove adapter sequences, low-quality bases (Q < 20), and contamination.

5. **Alignment / Mapping**
   - Map reads to a reference genome using aligners:
     - **TopHat / HISAT2 / STAR** for RNA-Seq
     - Microarrays skip this — they use pre-aligned probe sets.

6. **Quantification**
   - Count the number of reads (fragments) mapping to each gene/transcript.
   - Output: a **count matrix** (genes × samples).

7. **Normalization**
   - Correct for sequencing depth and gene length differences.
   - RPKM, FPKM, TPM transformations.

8. **Differential Expression (DE) Analysis**
   - Compare expression between conditions (e.g., treated vs. control).
   - Tools: **DESeq2, edgeR, limma-voom**.
   - Output: fold change, p-value, adjusted p-value (FDR via Benjamini-Hochberg).

9. **Functional Annotation & Pathway Enrichment**
   - Identify biological meaning using **GO (Gene Ontology)** and **KEGG pathways**.
   - Tools: **DAVID, clusterProfiler, GSEA**.

10. **Visualization**
    - **Volcano plots**, **MA plots**, **heatmaps**, **PCA plots**, **Venn diagrams**.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master these formulas — they are **frequently asked in KTU ESE questions**.

| Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables Explained | Use Case |
|--------------|------------------------|---------------------|----------|
| **Phred Quality Score** | $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P)$ | $P$ = probability of base-calling error | Quality filtering of reads |
| **RPKM** | $\text{RPKM}_i = \dfrac{C_i}{L_i \times N} \times 10^9$ | $C_i$ = reads mapped to gene $i$, $L_i$ = gene length (bp), $N$ = total mapped reads | Single-end RNA-Seq normalization |
| **FPKM** | $\text{FPKM}_i = \dfrac{F_i}{L_i \times N} \times 10^9$ | $F_i$ = fragment count, $L_i$ = gene length, $N$ = total fragments | Paired-end RNA-Seq normalization |
| **TPM (Step 1: RPK)** | $\text{RPK}_i = \dfrac{C_i}{L_i}$ | Count per kilobase | Intermediate step for TPM |
| **TPM (Step 2: Scaling)** | $\text{TPM}_i = \dfrac{\text{RPK}_i}{\sum_j \text{RPK}_j} \times 10^6$ | Normalized per million total | Modern RNA-Seq normalization |
| **Fold Change** | $\text{FC} = \dfrac{\text{Expression}_{\text{treated}}}{\text{Expression}_{\text{control}}}$ | Ratio of expression | Differential expression |
| **Log₂ Fold Change** | $\log_2 \text{FC} = \log_2 \left( \dfrac{E_T}{E_C} \right)$ | Log-transformed FC | Symmetric up/down regulation |
| **Benjamini-Hochberg FDR** | $p_{(i)}^{\text{adj}} = \min \left( p_{(i)} \times \dfrac{m}{i}, \; 1 \right)$ | $m$ = total tests, $i$ = rank | Multiple testing correction |
| **t-statistic (limma)** | $t_g = \dfrac{\bar{x}_g - \bar{y}_g}{s_g \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n_1} + \dfrac{1}{n_2}}}$ | Mean expression difference ÷ standard error | Significance testing |
| **Shannon Entropy** | $H = -\sum_i p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $p_i$ = probability of state $i$ | Diversity of expression |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE:** In all formulas above, the vertical bar `|` symbol is **strictly prohibited** inside markdown tables. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value or conditional notation.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Biomedical Utility

- **Drug Discovery:** Identify disease-associated genes as therapeutic targets (e.g., HER2 in breast cancer).
- **Personalized Medicine:** Tumor profiling to select targeted therapy.
- **Agricultural Biotechnology:** Identify stress-responsive genes in crops (drought, salinity tolerance).
- **Microbial Engineering:** Optimize metabolic pathways in industrial microorganisms.
- **Biomarker Discovery:** Detect diagnostic/prognostic markers in blood, saliva, or tissue.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the RPKM Normalization Formula

The RPKM formula is **derived from first principles** to ensure comparability across samples and genes of different lengths. Let us derive it step-by-step.

**Step 1: Problem Setup**

We have $N$ total reads mapped to the entire transcriptome. For a particular gene $i$:
- It receives $C_i$ reads.
- It has a length of $L_i$ bases (typically in kilobases).

**Step 2: Normalize for Gene Length**

Longer genes naturally accumulate more reads simply because they have more bases to which reads can map. To remove this length bias, we divide the read count by the gene length:

$$\text{Reads per Kilobase (RPK)} = \frac{C_i}{L_i}$$

**Step 3: Normalize for Sequencing Depth**

Different sequencing runs (samples) produce different total read counts. A sample with 50 million reads will have higher raw counts than one with 10 million reads, simply due to depth. To compare across samples, we scale by total reads $N$ (in millions):

$$\text{RPKM} = \frac{C_i}{L_i \times N}$$

Here, $L_i$ is in kilobases and $N$ is in millions of reads. The product $L_i \times N$ has units of (kb × million reads), so RPKM is in units of $\dfrac{\text{reads}}{\text{kb} \times \text{million reads}}$.

**Step 4: Per-Sample Scaling Factor**

To express RPKM in conventional units (reads per kb per million), we multiply by $10^9$ when $L_i$ is in bases and $N$ is in raw counts:

$$\text{RPKM}_i = \frac{C_i \times 10^9}{L_i \times N}$$

If $L_i$ is already in **kilobases** and $N$ is already in **millions**, then the formula is:

$$\text{RPKM}_i = \frac{C_i}{L_i \times N}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of TPM from RPKM

**TPM** is a length-normalized measure that ensures the sum of all TPM values within a sample equals exactly **1,000,000** — making it comparable across samples.

**Step 1: Compute RPK for every gene**

For each gene $j$ in the sample, compute:

$$\text{RPK}_j = \frac{C_j}{L_j}$$

**Step 2: Compute the per-sample scaling factor**

Sum the RPK values across **all genes** in the sample to get a per-million scaling factor:

$$S = \frac{1}{\sum_{j=1}^{G} \text{RPK}_j}$$

**Step 3: Convert each gene to TPM**

Scale every RPK by the same factor $S$ to get TPM, then multiply by $10^6$:

$$\text{TPM}_i = \frac{\text{RPK}_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{G} \text{RPK}_j} \times 10^6$$

**Verification:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{G} \text{TPM}_i = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{G} \text{RPK}_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{G} \text{RPK}_j} \times 10^6 = 1 \times 10^6 = 1{,}000{,}000$$

This confirms that TPM values sum to **one million per sample**, providing a clean probabilistic interpretation.

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example

**Problem:** A gene has $C_i = 1500$ reads mapped. Its length is $L_i = 2000$ bp. The total reads in the sample is $N = 10{,}000{,}000$. Calculate RPKM.

**Solution:**

Convert $L_i$ to kilobases: $L_i = 2000 \text{ bp} = 2 \text{ kb}$

Convert $N$ to millions: $N = 10{,}000{,}000 = 10$ million reads

Apply the formula:

$$\text{RPKM} = \frac{C_i}{L_i \times N} = \frac{1500}{2 \times 10} = \frac{1500}{20} = 75$$

The gene has an expression level of **75 RPKM**.

**Worked Example 2 — TPM Conversion:**

Suppose we have 3 genes with RPK values: $\text{RPK}_1 = 100$, $\text{RPK}_2 = 300$, $\text{RPK}_3 = 600$.

Total RPK: $\sum \text{RPK}_j = 100 + 300 + 600 = 1000$

Compute TPM for each:

$$\text{TPM}_1 = \frac{100}{1000} \times 10^6 = 100{,}000$$

$$\text{TPM}_2 = \frac{300}{1000} \times 10^6 = 300{,}000$$

$$\text{TPM}_3 = \frac{600}{1000} \times 10^6 = 600{,}000$$

Verification: $\sum \text{TPM} = 100{,}000 + 300{,}000 + 600{,}000 = 1{,}000{,}000$ ✓

### 3.4 Differential Expression Statistical Logic

To test whether a gene is **differentially expressed** between two conditions (e.g., disease vs. healthy), the **null hypothesis** $H_0$ states: *there is no difference in mean expression between the two groups*.

The **alternative hypothesis** $H_1$ states: *the means are different*.

The general linear model used in **limma-voom** is:

$$\log_2(\text{Expression}_{gi}) = \beta_{g0} + \beta_{g1} x_i + \epsilon_{gi}$$

where $g$ indexes the gene, $i$ indexes the sample, $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$ denotes the condition, and $\epsilon$ is the error term. The coefficient $\beta_{g1}$ represents the **log₂ fold change** for gene $g$.

The moderated t-statistic is:

$$t_g = \frac{\hat{\beta}_{g1}}{s_g \sqrt{v_g}}$$

where $s_g^2 v_g$ is the estimated variance of the coefficient. Genes with large $\vert t_g \vert$ and small $p$-value are declared differentially expressed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define gene expression analysis. Mention any two high-throughput techniques used for it.*

**Model Answer:**
Gene expression analysis is the study of measuring the activity of thousands of genes simultaneously to understand cellular function and response. The two main high-throughput techniques are:
1. **DNA Microarray** — uses hybridization of labeled cDNA to gene-specific probes on a solid surface.
2. **RNA-Seq** — uses next-generation sequencing of cDNA fragments to quantify transcripts.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark each for the two techniques)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*What is TPM? How is it different from RPKM?*

**Model Answer:**
**TPM (Transcripts Per Million)** is a normalization unit for RNA-Seq that accounts for both gene length and sequencing depth, such that TPM values sum to 1,000,000 per sample.

**Difference from RPKM:**
- In RPKM, the sum of values varies across samples; in TPM, the sum is always 1,000,000.
- TPM is **sample-independent** for comparing expression of the same gene across samples, making it the preferred metric in modern RNA-Seq studies.

*(3 marks: 1 mark TPM definition + 2 marks for the difference)*

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**(a)** Explain the complete workflow of RNA-Seq based gene expression analysis with a neat block diagram. Highlight the role of quality control and normalization in the process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the mathematical formula for RPKM. Given a gene of length 2.5 kb that received 800 reads in a sample with a total of 20 million mapped reads, calculate the RPKM value. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The RNA-Seq workflow consists of the following sequential steps:

1. **Sample Preparation:** Total RNA is extracted, and mRNA is enriched using poly-A tail capture. RNA integrity is verified using **Bioanalyzer (RIN ≥ 7.0)**.

2. **Library Preparation:** mRNA is fragmented, reverse-transcribed into cDNA, and sequencing adapters are ligated.

3. **Sequencing:** The library is sequenced on a high-throughput platform (e.g., Illumina NovaSeq), generating millions of short reads stored as **FASTQ files**.

4. **Quality Control:** Tools like **FASTQC** assess per-base quality, GC content, and adapter contamination. **Trimmomatic** or **fastp** removes low-quality bases and adapters.

5. **Alignment:** Reads are mapped to a reference genome using splice-aware aligners such as **HISAT2** or **STAR**, producing SAM/BAM files.

6. **Quantification:** Mapped reads are assigned to genes using **HTSeq-count** or **featureCounts**, producing a raw count matrix.

7. **Normalization:** Raw counts are normalized to **TPM** or using **DESeq2 size factors** to remove technical bias.

8. **Differential Expression:** Statistical tools (**DESeq2, edgeR, limma-voom**) compute fold change and p-values.

9. **Visualization & Interpretation:** **Volcano plots, heatmaps, GO/KEGG enrichment** provide biological meaning.

**Role of Quality Control:** QC removes erroneous bases that would otherwise create false alignments and misleading counts, ensuring reproducible results.

**Role of Normalization:** Normalization corrects for differences in gene length and sequencing depth, making expression values comparable across genes and samples.

*(7 marks distributed: 1 mark per major step x 5 = 5 marks + 1 mark QC + 1 mark Normalization)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Derivation:**

Let $C_i$ be the number of reads mapped to gene $i$, $L_i$ its length in kilobases, and $N$ the total number of mapped reads in millions.

The reads per kilobase (RPK) normalizes for gene length:

$$\text{RPK} = \frac{C_i}{L_i}$$

Dividing by total reads $N$ (in millions) normalizes for sequencing depth:

$$\text{RPKM} = \frac{C_i}{L_i \times N}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given:
- $C_i = 800$ reads
- $L_i = 2.5$ kb
- $N = 20$ million reads

$$\text{RPKM} = \frac{800}{2.5 \times 20} = \frac{800}{50} = 16$$

The RPKM value is **16**.

*(7 marks: 3 marks derivation + 2 marks formula application + 2 marks numerical answer)*

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Compare DNA Microarray and RNA-Seq technologies for gene expression analysis. Mention at least five points of comparison. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is differential gene expression? Explain the Benjamini-Hochberg method for multiple testing correction with a suitable formula. Why is it necessary in gene expression studies? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

| Comparison Parameter | DNA Microarray | RNA-Seq |
|----------------------|----------------|---------|
| **Principle** | Hybridization to pre-designed probes | Sequencing of cDNA fragments |
| **Dynamic Range** | Limited (~100-fold) | Wide (>10,000-fold) |
| **Background Noise** | High (cross-hybridization) | Low (digital signal) |
| **Resolution** | Gene-level | Single-nucleotide, isoform-level |
| **Discovery of Novel Transcripts** | Not possible | Possible |
| **Cost per Sample** | Lower | Higher (decreasing) |
| **Required Amount of RNA** | Higher | Lower |
| **Reference Required** | Yes (pre-designed chip) | Yes (for alignment) |

*(7 marks: 1 mark per comparison point, 5 main points + 2 supporting points)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Definition:** Differential gene expression refers to the identification of genes whose expression levels differ significantly between two or more biological conditions (e.g., tumor vs. normal).

**Benjamini-Hochberg (BH) Method:**

When testing $m$ genes simultaneously, the family-wise error rate inflates. The BH method controls the **False Discovery Rate (FDR)** — the expected proportion of false positives among the rejected null hypotheses.

**Procedure:**
1. Order the $m$ p-values in ascending order: $p_{(1)} \leq p_{(2)} \leq \cdots \leq p_{(m)}$.
2. For each rank $i$, compute the adjusted p-value:

$$p_{(i)}^{\text{adj}} = \min \left( p_{(i)} \times \frac{m}{i}, \; 1 \right)$$

3. Enforce monotonicity: ensure that $p_{(i)}^{\text{adj}} \geq p_{(i-1)}^{\text{adj}}$ for all $i$.

**Why is it necessary?**
In a typical gene expression experiment, **thousands of genes** are tested simultaneously. If a p-value threshold of 0.05 is used naively, then 5% of all tested genes will be false positives by chance. For 20,000 genes, this yields **1,000 false discoveries**. The BH method ensures that the **proportion of false discoveries** among the declared significant genes is bounded by the chosen FDR level (e.g., 5%).

*(7 marks: 1 mark definition + 3 marks BH formula and procedure + 3 marks necessity explanation)*

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in Gene Expression Analysis Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing RPKM with FPKM:** RPKM is for single-end reads, FPKM is for paired-end fragments. Examiners will deduct 1 mark if you interchange them.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units:** In RPKM calculations, students often forget to convert gene length to **kilobases** and total reads to **millions**. Always state unit conversions explicitly.
> 3. **TPM misinterpretation:** Saying "TPM normalizes within a sample only" is wrong — TPM is also comparable **across samples**, which is its key advantage.
> 4. **Microarray vs. RNA-Seq:** Writing that microarrays "sequence RNA" is incorrect. Microarrays use **hybridization**, not sequencing.
> 5. **Missing the FDR concept:** Examiners expect you to mention that FDR controls the **expected proportion of false positives** among significant genes, not the family-wise error rate.
> 6. **Skipping the p-value adjustment step:** Always mention that p-values must be **corrected for multiple testing** before declaring significance.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Gene Expression Analysis:**

- **Core Definition:** Gene expression analysis quantifies mRNA abundance across samples to identify differentially expressed genes.
- **Two Major Platforms:** DNA Microarray (hybridization) and RNA-Seq (sequencing). RNA-Seq has wider dynamic range and detects novel transcripts.
- **Workflow Steps:** Sample → RNA extraction → Library prep → Sequencing/Hybridization → QC → Alignment → Counting → Normalization → DE analysis → Functional annotation → Visualization.
- **RPKM Formula:** $\text{RPKM}_i = \dfrac{C_i}{L_i \times N}$ — normalize by gene length and sequencing depth.
- **FPKM Formula:** Same as RPKM but uses **fragment count** (paired-end reads).
- **TPM Formula:** $\text{TPM}_i = \dfrac{\text{RPK}_i}{\sum_j \text{RPK}_j} \times 10^6$ — TPM values sum to 1,000,000 per sample.
- **Fold Change:** Ratio of treated to control expression; **log₂FC** is preferred for symmetry.
- **Significance Threshold:** Adjusted p-value (FDR) **< 0.05** and **|log₂FC| ≥ 1** is the standard cutoff.
- **Benjamini-Hochberg FDR:** $p_{(i)}^{\text{adj}} = p_{(i)} \times \dfrac{m}{i}$ (with monotonicity enforcement).
- **Visualization Tools:** Volcano plot (significance vs. magnitude), MA plot (intensity vs. fold change), heatmap (clustering of samples and genes), PCA (sample similarity).
- **Functional Enrichment Databases:** Gene Ontology (GO) and KEGG pathways provide biological interpretation of DEGs.
- **Common Tools:** DESeq2, edgeR, limma-voom (DE analysis); HISAT2, STAR (alignment); HTSeq, featureCounts (counting); clusterProfiler, DAVID (enrichment).
- **Phred Quality Score:** $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P)$ — Q20 means 1% error rate; Q30 means 0.1% error rate.
